name: Brilliant
overview: Brilliant is a STEM (math/science/CS) learning app founded in 2012 that teaches one concept at a time through 15-minute interactive lessons. Each problem is built as a hands-on visual manipulation rather than text/video, with immediate custom feedback. Premium is $24.99/month or $13.49/month annual. Their 'Game Feel' redesign with ustwo and Rive-powered animations (streaks, learning paths) significantly lifted day-over-day engagement, especially on mobile.
originator_or_company: Brilliant Worldwide, Inc. (San Francisco). Co-founded in 2012 by Sue Khim (CEO, ex-University of Chicago); design VP Peter Cho led the recent visual identity. ~114 employees, ~$14.3M revenue (2024).
key_resources: [ustwo case study — Brilliant Game Feel North Star](https://ustwo.com/work/brilliant/)
[Rive blog — How Brilliant.org motivates learners with Rive animations](https://rive.app/blog/how-brilliant-org-motivates-learners-with-rive-animations)
core_mechanics: 1) One-concept-per-lesson structure: each lesson is ~15 minutes and isolates a single idea, building intuition through visual manipulation before formal procedure. 2) Direct instruction + blocked problem solving: short explainer, then a sequence of similar problems with custom, intelligent feedback that catches specific mistake patterns. 3) Visual/interactive primitives instead of text+video: drag sliders, tap nodes, manipulate diagrams; users 'do' rather than 'watch'. 4) Adaptive practice sets with spaced repetition. 5) Game Feel layer (ustwo collaboration, ~10-week project): a Level Gameboard for lesson progress, a learning companion that points to the next lesson, in-lesson celebrations for correct answers, encouragement moments when struggling, and a shared visual library of tiles, tooltips, progress trackers. 6) Rive-driven motion: every learning-path node and connecting line is a Rive animation with per-topic color variants; the streak counter is wired to Rive Event triggers so the animation increments in lock-step with the number; same Rive file ships to iOS, Android, and Web (one fix = three platforms). 7) Habit scaffolding: streaks, leagues, leveling — explicitly tuned to support habit formation without overwhelming the content. 8) Free foundational access + Premium paywall ($24.99/mo or $161.88/yr ≈ $13.49/mo) that unlocks all courses, all quizzes, and offline mode; 7-day free trial.
designer_or_researcher_voice: Peter Cho (VP of Design, Brilliant): 'ustwo were collaborative, creative partners you dream to have. They pushed our thinking in early stages, helping us imagine expansive new ways the "game" of Brilliant could be fun and surprising.' (ustwo case study)
Zack Davenport (Lead Product Designer, Brilliant) on Rive: 'There is definitely a one-to-one that what you build in Rive is what's in the product.' (Rive blog)
Juliana Chen (Illustrator, Brilliant): 'I saved a full day of work using Rive because I didn't have to do all the prep work and cross-check for handoff anymore.' (Rive blog)
Brilliant About page (org voice): 'Realizing your mind's potential is a satisfying pursuit in itself' — framing problem solving as intrinsically motivating, not as a means to a job outcome.
Brilliant pedagogy doc (org voice): each lesson 'first builds intuition with visual explanations, hands-on manipulation, and concrete computation' before introducing formal notation.
metrics_or_evidence: ~10M+ registered users (Brilliant marketing copy). ~$14.3M revenue, 114 employees in 2024 (Latka). brilliant.org ~2.9M monthly visits, avg session 7m 43s, global rank ~17,923 (Similarweb, late 2025). Direct traffic 50.7%, organic search 32.3%; top countries US > India > UK. Post-Game-Feel redesign: ustwo reports 'more people engaging day over day and week over week, and continuing toward their learning goals, especially in mobile apps' (qualitative — no public delta % published). Premium price points: $24.99/mo, $161.88/yr ($13.49/mo equivalent), 7-day trial.
criticisms: Limited subject scope (math/science/CS only) — narrow vs Coursera/edX. Interactivity is sometimes shallow ('mostly sliders'); critics argue many 'interactive' steps are just multiple-choice with a manipulator skin. Coding/CS tracks read more like brain-teaser/interview prep than how to ship real apps. No completion certificates — limits usefulness for résumé-driven learners. Pricing perceived as steep at $24.99/mo for STEM-only content. Personalization beyond difficulty adjustment is thin. Possible novelty-effect: the Game Feel layer drives engagement but long-term skill transfer to formal exams is not publicly substantiated.
failure_cases: Documented user complaints (Trustpilot/PissedConsumer): unauthorized charges after canceling during the 7-day trial, slow/unresponsive customer service on refunds, difficulty locating the cancel flow. The original pre-2023 product was reportedly more text/competition-math heavy (problem-of-the-week community); Brilliant pivoted away from that toward bite-sized interactive courses, effectively retiring the community-competition identity. No major public post-mortem of dropped features, but the pivot itself is a deprecation.
antipatterns: 1) Streak-as-pressure: importing Duolingo-style streak panic into a paid STEM context can manufacture anxiety more than learning — the streak should celebrate, not punish. 2) Game Feel covering for thin pedagogy: animations and celebrations cannot fix a lesson that isn't actually building intuition; risk of 'sugar coating' a confused mental model. 3) Means-Ends Reversal: optimizing for daily app opens rather than concept mastery — Brilliant explicitly guards against this by keeping lessons 15 min and concept-isolated, but the pattern is easy to copy badly. 4) Trial-to-paid dark patterns: hard-to-find cancel flows and silent auto-renewal are the consumer complaint surface — do not replicate. 5) Over-gamification of high-stakes content: cute companion characters can read as condescending to adult professional learners (shikaq's 20s-women career-track persona).
applicability_to_shikaq: Highly applicable, with adaptation. (a) One-concept-per-lesson + 15-minute frame maps cleanly onto shikaq's IT Passport / 基本情報 / SPI / 簿記2級 syllabus chunks — each 単元 becomes one Brilliant-style 'Learn → 3-5 interactive problems → custom feedback' loop, fitting the cafe/iPad片手 use case. (b) Replace text-heavy 参考書 segments with hands-on manipulators: drag bits to visualize 2進数, tap rows of a 仕訳 to balance借方/貸方, slide a clock to compute 利息. This is the explicit anti-参考書 wedge shikaq's vision describes. (c) Game Feel layer can be implemented via Rive on RN (rive-react-native) so iOS/iPadOS/Web share one source of truth — matches our Apple-HIG-only stance because Rive output is just a vector layer we can drop into BlurView/GlassView frames. (d) Streak + learning-path Rive animations fit the Apple Fitness-style 3-ring metaphor already in `ActivityRings.tsx` — extend ActivityRings for daily problem-count celebration. (e) HEXAD: serves Achievers (mastery via ring-closing), Free Spirits (path exploration), Players (streak/level rewards). Less optimized for Socializers (Brilliant has no social layer) — we should not import that gap. (f) Pricing translation: ¥980/mo is ~1/3 of Brilliant's monthly — the unit economics force us to lean even harder on AI-generated problems (DeepSeek + Obsidian RAG) rather than hand-authored Rive animations per concept; reuse Rive primitives (number-up animation, node-color variants) across all 4 資格.
phase_assignment: Phase 2 (4-6 weeks). Phase 1 should ship the static one-concept lesson + custom-feedback loop without animation polish. Phase 2 introduces the Rive-powered streak ring + learning-path nodes and the in-lesson celebration moments. Phase 3 layers adaptive practice and the AI-generated companion encouragement.
integration_points: Components: extend `TodayContent.tsx` with a Brilliant-style 'next lesson' card and learning-path widget; promote `ActivityRings.tsx` / `GoalRing.tsx` into the streak celebration (Rive event-triggered). New `components/screens/Lesson.tsx` runs the 1-concept loop. New `components/InteractiveProblem.tsx` for slider/tap/drag manipulators. `MaterialIcon.tsx` stays for chrome icons (Apple HIG); Rive only for in-lesson celebration + path nodes. DB: `lessons.content_type = 'quiz'` or new `'interactive'` enum value; `questions.format` already supports `multiple_choice`/`written`/`cbt` — add metadata jsonb on `questions` for manipulator config. Track engagement on `progress` (lesson_completed_at) and a new `streaks` table (user_id, current_streak, longest_streak, last_active_date). Routes: `/lesson/[id]` (already in repo as deleted route — re-introduce). Edge function for adaptive next-lesson selection.
risks: 1) Implementation cost: Rive authoring requires a designer who can build state-machine animations — current shikaq team is one-person; mitigation = reuse Brilliant-style primitives across all 4 資格 and start with 3 hero animations (streak ring, correct-answer burst, path node). 2) Apple-HIG conflict: cute mascot/companion would clash with our Apple-純正 stance. Mitigation = use a geometric, abstract celebration (Apple Fitness ring close) instead of a character. 3) Persona mismatch: 20代女性キャリア層 may read companion characters as childish; keep all motion restrained, Liquid Glass / SF Pro typography, no kawaii. 4) Content production: Brilliant has IMO medalists hand-authoring problems — we are using AI + Obsidian RAG; risk of shallower 'feels interactive but isn't' problems. Mitigation = Editor校閲 gate (questions.status draft→published) before any problem ships. 5) Scope creep: Brilliant ships 90+ courses; we must resist building a Brilliant clone and stay focused on 4 資格. 6) Streak anxiety / dark-pattern risk on a paid app — design streak as celebration only, never punishment, and offer pause days.
apple_hig_compatibility: needs_adaptation. Brilliant's Game Feel direction (cute companion, color explosion celebrations, gameboard tiles) is more Duolingo-adjacent than Apple-HIG-pure. However, the underlying ideas — 1-concept-per-screen, Deference (UI gives way to the manipulator), Depth (Rive layers stack like Liquid Glass), Spring motion, restrained celebration — are fully compatible with Apple HIG once we strip the mascot and replace bright fills with semantic color + Liquid Glass. Specifically: keep the path-node + streak-number Rive ideas, drop the character; map celebrations to Apple Fitness ring-close style (radial sweep + haptic .notification.success).
ethical_score: neutral. Positive: intrinsic-motivation framing ('mind's potential is satisfying in itself'), 15-min ceiling caps usage, no infinite-scroll feed, no social comparison leaderboards by default. Negative: streaks can manufacture anxiety; cancel-flow complaints suggest some growth-hack creep; $24.99/mo can lock learners into sunk-cost behavior. Net: design intent is humane but execution drifts toward standard SaaS retention dark patterns at the edges. shikaq must explicitly defend against the cancel-flow drift and offer streak-pause / vacation-mode from day one.
evidence_quality: company_case_study (ustwo + Rive blog post + Brilliant's own About page), supplemented by anecdote (Trustpilot reviews) and observational_study (Similarweb / Latka traffic & revenue figures). No rigorous_RCT on Brilliant's pedagogical claims is publicly available.
localization_fit_japan: medium. Pros: 20代女性キャリア層 already accept short-form mobile learning (e.g. スタディサプリ, NewsPicks, mikan); the 'one concept, 15 minutes, on iPad in cafe' pattern matches the persona perfectly; visual manipulators reduce the cognitive cost of dense Japanese 専門用語 in the early intuition stage. Cons: Brilliant is English/STEM-only with no Japanese localization; the 'leveling/companion' aesthetic skews male-tech-bro and would need full revisual for the persona; 資格 culture in Japan is exam-outcome-driven (合格 vs 不合格) more than 'mastery for its own sake', so pure intrinsic-motivation framing must be augmented with concrete 過去問正答率 / 合格圏内 dashboards.
